rulings.Case Title: GLENN MENSING VS. LYNDON LIU et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Defts Further Answer Pltf'S Special Interrogatories And For Monetary Sanction And Other Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday June 30, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF GLENN MENSING'S Motion To Compel Defendant's Further Answer to Plaintiff's Special Interrogatories And For Monetary Sanction And Other Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Moving party, Glenn Mensing is ORDERED to appear and bring with him to court the original responses of Defendant Heidi De Los Reyes to Plaintiff's Special Interrogatories, Set One. The Responses attached to Plaintiff's brief to which he seeks a further response to Special Interrogatories 6, 9,10,12,18, 25, 27 and 33 are undated, unsigned, unverified and there is no proof of service attached. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org and peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm the court day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare an order repeating verbatim the substantive portion. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: KIMBERLY SWAN VS. EPISCOPAL COMMUNITY SERVICES
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Response To Interrogatory And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday June 30, 2015, Line 4, PLAINTIFF KIMBERLY SWAN'S Motion To Compel Further Response To Interrogatory And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Van Zandt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Kimberly Swan's Motion to Compel Further Interrogatory Responses and for Sanctions is DENIED. The Motion seeks to compel Defendant Episcopal Community Services of San Francisco to disclose the "address and telephone numbers of Rasheedah Blake", a former employee of Defendant. It is well established that a former employee has a high expectation of privacy related to personal information in their personnel file. (County of Los Angeles v. Los Angeles County Employee Relations Commission (2013), 56 Cal.4th 905) Defendant has established all 3 essential elements necessary for the Court to deny Plaintiff's motion---(1) legally protected privacy right, (2)objective and reasonable expectation of claimant's privacy, and (3)invasion of privacy serious in nature and scope. (Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1994), 7 Cal.4th 1) The Court has balanced the former employee's privacy rights as against the Plaintiff's need for disclosure and finds that Plaintiff must look elsewhere for this information. The sanction requests from both sides are DENIED. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org and peter.vanzandt@leclairryan.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm the court day prior to the hearing stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem Judge hearing the motion and the Pro Tem Judge will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare an order repeating verbatim the substantive portion. =(302/JPT)
rulings.Judge: Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt

rulings.Case Title: K BRIAN ROMANESCHI VS. SASIMU, LLC A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 30, 2015, Line 9, DEFENDANT SARIT SIMHAYOFF-COHEN'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. Denied. CCP sec. 412.20 governs the content of a summons directed at an individual defendant. There is no requirement in section 412.20 that a summons explicitly states the name of the individual defendant being served with the summons; rather, the only requirement is that the summons states the names of the parties in the action, which the summons in this case did. (Declaration of Sarit Simhayoff Cohen, Ex. A.) Additionally, CCP sec. 412.20(c) provides that "[a] summons in a form approved by the Judicial Council is deemed to comply with this section." Plaintiff used a Judicial Council Form Summons. (Cohen Decl., Ex. A.) The Judicial Council Form only requires the plaintiff to check a box stating that "You are being served as an individual defendant." There is no requirement on the Judicial Council Form that the individual defendant be identified by name next to where the box is checked. Further, Mannesmann Demag, Ltd. v. Sup. Ct. (1985) 172 Cal.App.3d 1118, 1122, cited by Defendant, is inapplicable. That case discussed CCP sec. 420.30, which explicitly requires that a summons identify a corporate defendant by name. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JMQ)
